questions.prompt: How does Storage enforce authorization for private buckets?

choices.text: RLS policies on the `storage.objects` table. Policies check `bucket_id` and use `storage.foldername(name)` to extract path components — typical pattern is to encode the owner's user_id as the first folder (avatars/{user_id}/...) and check `(storage.foldername(name))[1] = auth.uid()::text`. Same RLS engine as your data tables; same security model. Subscribers, uploaders, and viewers all run through the policies; the database denies what isn't allowed.

questions.explanation: RLS on storage.objects with path-based checks via storage.foldername().

questions.prompt: Need to serve resized avatars (200x200) without storing every size. Approach?

choices.text: Use on-the-fly image transformations via getPublicUrl/createSignedUrl with the `transform` option: `{ width: 200, height: 200, resize: 'cover', format: 'webp', quality: 80 }`. Storage processes the transformation on the edge and caches the result at the CDN; subsequent requests for the same transform are fast. You store one high-res master per file and serve any size on demand. Saves storage cost (one source) and bandwidth (modern formats like webp). Particularly useful for responsive images at multiple device sizes.

questions.explanation: On-the-fly transformations + edge-cache. Store one master, serve many sizes.
